name: Royal Archive
description: The Royal Archive is a cathedral of paperwork. Shelves stretch from floor to ceiling, packed with scrolls, ledgers, and documents bound in leather that predates several kings. The air smells of old parchment and institutional authority. Dust motes float in shafts of grey light from narrow windows. Many shelves are turning to stone -- their contents petrifying mid-sentence, preserving laws and decrees in a medium considerably more permanent than parchment. A section labeled 'CURSES, HEXES, AND RELATED GRIEVANCES (Filing Section C-7)' catches your eye.

responses.look: Section C-7: Curses, Hexes, and Related Grievances. The shelves are organized by date, severity, and petulance level of the caster. Most are marked 'Resolved.' One near the back is marked 'ACTIVE -- DO NOT TAMPER.' Its edges are singed, which is either a filing notation or a warning.
responses.use: You rifle through the curse filings. Most are mundane -- cursed cow, hexed well, a very specific grudge against someone's turnip crop. The active one is filed at the very back, behind a stack of resolved hexes.
responses.take: You can't take the entire filing section. Even if you could carry it, the archive's organizational system would never recover.
responses.default: You examine the curse filing section. It's surprisingly well-organized for a topic that involves screaming wizards and malevolent incantations.
responses.talk: You address Section C-7 directly, which is a sentence that should concern you. The curses don't talk back, though one filing rustles slightly. That's either a draft or a grievance expressing itself.
responses.open: The section is already open. That's how filing works. There's no dramatic reveal here -- just alphabetized misery.
responses.push: You shove the filing section. The shelves creak but hold. Centuries of curses aren't easily displaced. Much like the curses themselves.
responses.pull: You tug at the filing section. It shifts an inch, revealing a cobweb and the skeleton of a mouse that presumably died of boredom while reading tax hexes.

responses.look: Half the archive has turned to stone. Scrolls and their shelves alike, frozen in grey. You can read the stone text if you look closely -- laws about taxes, grazing rights, and a surprisingly detailed regulation about the acceptable width of hats. The kingdom's bureaucracy is preserved forever. Whether that's a loss or a mercy is debatable.
responses.take: You try to pull a stone scroll from the shelf. It snaps. Centuries of legal precedent, shattered. The kingdom's lawyers would weep, if they weren't also stone.
responses.default: You study the stone shelves. The petrification is indiscriminate in its destruction but oddly respectful of filing order.
responses.use: Using the Stone Shelves doesn't accomplish anything obvious. Perhaps it needs to be used with something else. Or perhaps it's just decorative.
responses.talk: You speak to the stone shelves. They have the same conversational range as most legal texts -- absolute silence, radiating quiet authority.
responses.open: The shelves are open. They were open when they turned to stone, and they'll be open when the sun burns out. Petrification is very thorough about preserving the status quo.
responses.push: You push against the stone shelves. They don't budge. Stone is famously indifferent to human ambition.
responses.pull: You pull at a petrified shelf. Stone grinds against stone. A chunk of mineral-shelf crumbles in your hand, releasing the dust of fossilized knowledge.

responses.look: The archivist's desk is half-stone, the chair pushed back as though the archivist left in a hurry. A mug of tea sits petrified mid-steam. Notes on the desk read: 'Curse contract -- Section C-7, shelf 3, row 12. DO NOT file under Miscellaneous. The auditor was very clear about this.'
responses.use: You sit at the archivist's desk. For a moment, you feel the weight of centuries of filing. It is not a pleasant feeling. You stand up.
responses.default: You examine the archivist's desk. The petrified tea is a monument to interrupted work.
responses.take: The desk is half-petrified and weighs approximately as much as your life choices. It's not going anywhere, and neither are you if you keep trying to lift it.
responses.talk: You address the empty desk as though the archivist might still be listening. 'Hello? Anyone?' The petrified tea steams in eternal silence.
responses.open: You pull at the desk drawer. It's half-stone, so it opens about two inches before jamming. Inside: a quill, a dried-out inkwell, and a note reading 'GONE FOR TEA BREAK -- BACK IN 5 MIN.' That was several centuries ago.
responses.push: You push the desk. The stone half doesn't move. The wooden half scrapes forward an inch, making a sound like a dying cat.
responses.pull: You pull the desk toward you. The legs scrape against the floor with a noise that echoes through the archive like a scream in a library.

responses.look: Leather-bound tomes stacked in precarious towers, their spines cracked and titles faded. The kingdom's entire legal history, slowly becoming unreadable.
responses.take: You open one at random. It's a three-hundred-page treatise on hat regulations. You put it back.
responses.use: You flip through a tome. The pages crumble at the edges, taking centuries of case law with them.
responses.default: You examine the tomes. They hold answers to questions nobody alive remembers asking.
responses.talk: You read aloud from a random tome. 'Whereas the party of the first part did knowingly and with malice aforethought wear a hat exceeding the statutory width...' You stop. Some knowledge is better left unspoken.
responses.open: You crack open a tome. The spine protests with a sound like breaking knuckles. The page you land on details the legal proceedings of a man who was fined for owning too many geese.
responses.push: You push the stack of tomes. They topple in slow motion, each one hitting the floor with a thud that sends up a cloud of dust and crushed ambition.
responses.pull: You pull a tome from the middle of a stack. The rest hold, miraculously. The one you've extracted is titled 'On the Proper Governance of Puddings, Vol. III.'

responses.look: Narrow windows let in thin shafts of grey light, illuminating dust motes that drift like tiny ghosts through the archive's stale air.
responses.take: Windows are a structural feature, not a collectible.
responses.use: You peer through the narrow glass. The view is limited to a thin slice of a cursed kingdom, which is honestly enough.
responses.default: You glance at the narrow windows. They admit light grudgingly, as if rationing hope.
responses.talk: You whisper to the windows. The wind outside whistles back, which is more conversation than you've had with most things in this archive.
responses.open: The windows are sealed shut. Centuries of grime and neglect have fused frame to wall. The archive prefers its air stale, its light rationed, and its visitors mildly claustrophobic.
responses.push: You push against the window glass. It holds, though it makes a concerning creak. Breaking the only light source in a room full of petrified shelving seems like a plan with limited upside.
responses.pull: You pull at a window frame. The stone doesn't yield. These windows were built as much for defense as for light, and they defend themselves admirably.

responses.look: Tiny particles of centuries-old paper, dead skin, and pulverized law drift through the grey light. Each mote is a fragment of something that used to matter. Now they're just atmospheric.
responses.take: You wave your hand through the dust motes. They scatter, regroup, and resume drifting as though you were never here. You have been ignored by particles.
responses.use: You're not sure what you expected to accomplish by using dust. It's dust. It has one job -- existing aimlessly -- and it's very good at it.
responses.talk: You address the dust motes. They continue drifting in complete silence. To be fair, this is roughly the same response you'd get from most civil servants.
responses.open: You cannot open dust. This is not a concept that applies. The dust seems unbothered by your confusion.
responses.push: You swipe at the dust motes. They swirl dramatically for a moment, then settle back into their languid drift.
responses.pull: You cannot pull dust. It lacks handles, edges, and any cooperative spirit whatsoever.
responses.default: Dust motes drift through grey light. The archive's most active residents.

responses.look: A stone mug of tea, frozen mid-steam. The surface is perfectly preserved -- you can even see the ring where the tea stained the inside of the cup. The steam curls upward in solid grey, a permanent monument to someone's last tea break.
responses.take: You pick up the stone mug. It's heavier than expected. You put it back. Stealing a dead archivist's tea feels like a line you shouldn't cross, even if the tea is technically a mineral now.
responses.use: You raise the stone mug to your lips. Stone tea tastes exactly like you'd expect -- grey, hard, and profoundly unsatisfying.
responses.talk: You address the petrified tea. 'Good cuppa?' Silence. The tea has no opinions. It never will.
responses.open: It's a mug. It's already open. Mugs are philosophically committed to openness.
responses.push: You nudge the stone mug across the desk. It scrapes against the surface with a sound that makes your teeth hurt.
responses.pull: You drag the stone mug toward you. It leaves a scratch on the desk. The archivist would be furious, but the archivist is either stone or long gone.
responses.default: The petrified tea sits on the desk, forever mid-sip. The world's longest tea break.

responses.look: A wooden chair pushed back from the desk at an angle that suggests sudden departure. The seat is worn smooth by years of dedicated sitting. One armrest is stone; the rest is wood.
responses.take: You try to lift the chair. The stone armrest makes it absurdly heavy on one side. Moving half-petrified furniture is not a skill that appears on any quest checklist.
responses.use: You sit in the archivist's chair. It's surprisingly comfortable, if you ignore the stone armrest digging into your elbow. For one quiet moment you are the Royal Archivist.
responses.talk: You address the chair. It supports you, literally if not emotionally. This is more than can be said for most of the kingdom's infrastructure.
responses.open: Chairs don't open. This one especially doesn't, as it is partially stone and entirely uninterested in your creative interpretations of furniture.
responses.push: You push the chair. It rolls back slightly on the stone floor, then catches on a groove and stops.
responses.pull: You pull the chair toward you. The legs screech against the floor. The sound bounces off every stone surface and returns to you amplified.
responses.default: The archivist's chair. Pushed back as if someone left in a hurry. Centuries ago.

responses.look: Handwritten notes in cramped, precise script. The top page reads: 'Curse contract -- Section C-7, shelf 3, row 12. DO NOT file under Miscellaneous. The auditor was very clear about this.'
responses.take: You pocket the archivist's notes. They might be useful, or they might just be the ramblings of someone who spent too long alone with cursed paperwork.
responses.use: You read through the notes carefully. One margin note reads: 'If the curse gets worse, the contract is the key. Clause 47-B. I wrote it down somewhere. Check C-7.'
responses.talk: You read the notes aloud. Your voice echoes through the empty archive, giving the archivist's words an audience centuries too late.
responses.open: The notes are loose pages on a desk, not a sealed envelope. They're as open as information gets.
responses.push: You scatter the notes across the desk. Some flutter to the floor. You immediately feel guilty and stack them back up.
responses.pull: You slide the notes toward you. A page catches on the petrified tea mug and tears slightly. You wince.
responses.default: The archivist's notes. Someone was trying to help, even if nobody was listening.

responses.look: Individual scrolls turned to solid stone, their rolled parchment now grey cylinders of preserved text. One is half-unrolled, its text visible: '...and henceforth, the wearing of pointed shoes exceeding four inches shall be...' The sentence is cut off by petrification.
responses.take: You try to pick up a stone scroll. It's heavier than it looks and slips from your fingers, clunking back onto the shelf.
responses.use: You try to unroll a stone scroll. Unsurprisingly, stone does not unroll. You've essentially tried to read a rock.
responses.talk: You speak to the stone scrolls. They contain the frozen words of long-dead bureaucrats. In a way, you're having a conversation with history. In another way, you're talking to rocks.
responses.open: You try to unroll a petrified scroll. It does not unroll. It is stone. Stone does not unroll.
responses.push: You push a stone scroll. It rolls along the shelf with a grinding sound and bumps against its neighbor.
responses.pull: You pull a stone scroll from its position. The shelf groans warningly.
responses.default: Stone scrolls, frozen mid-decree. The bureaucracy, preserved for eternity.

responses.look: The leather bindings on the older tomes are cracked and dark with age, some predating the current dynasty. They smell of animal hide and the passage of time. A few have been stamped with royal seals from kings whose names are now footnotes.
responses.take: You peel a strip of leather from a binding. The tome it was protecting falls open, pages scattering like startled birds. You now own a piece of leather and a guilty conscience.
responses.use: You run your thumb along the leather. It's dry, cracked, and older than most family trees. The craftsmanship is remarkable.
responses.talk: You compliment the leather bindings on their durability. They do not respond, but they've lasted longer than every king who ever ruled here.
responses.open: The bindings are what hold the tomes closed. Opening them means opening the books, and you've already seen enough hat legislation for one lifetime.
responses.push: You press against the leather-bound spines. They're packed tight, shoulder to shoulder on the shelf like commuters who'd rather not make eye contact.
responses.pull: You tug at a leather binding. It comes away from its tome with a dry ripping sound. You've just defrocked a legal text.
responses.default: Cracked leather, older than dynasties. The bindings outlasted everything they were meant to protect.

responses.look: A heavy wax seal on the curse contract, stamped with an intricate sigil you don't recognize. The wax is dark red -- nearly black -- and warm to the touch, even after centuries. Whatever magic binds this contract is still alive.
responses.take: You try to pry the wax seal off the contract. It won't budge. The seal is as much a part of the document as the words themselves.
responses.use: You press your thumb against the wax seal. It's warm. A faint hum rises from the parchment, then fades. The contract acknowledges your touch and declines to comment.
responses.talk: You speak to the wax seal. It hums faintly in response, which is either magical resonance or the building settling.
responses.open: You can't open a wax seal without breaking the contract's binding magic. That seems like the kind of thing that would void the warranty.
responses.push: You press down on the wax seal. The warmth intensifies briefly, then subsides. The contract is aware of you.
responses.pull: You try to pull the wax seal free. It holds fast. Magical seals are not designed for casual removal.
responses.default: The wax seal. Dark, warm, and humming with old magic. The contract's signature.

responses.look: Handwritten labels on small cards, slotted into brass holders. The penmanship is immaculate. Sections include: 'A-1: Taxation (General),' 'C-7: Curses, Hexes, and Related Grievances,' 'D-12: Pudding Governance,' and 'Z-1: Miscellaneous (DO NOT USE -- SEE AUDITOR).'
responses.take: You slide a label from its holder. It reads 'C-7: Curses, Hexes, and Related Grievances.' You put it back. Removing the labels from a filing system is how civilizations fall.
responses.use: You trace the filing system with your finger. It's logical, thorough, and completely useless when the archive is half stone. But you appreciate the effort.
responses.talk: You read the labels aloud like a very boring tour guide. 'And here we have Section D-12, Pudding Governance...' Nobody is listening.
responses.open: The labels are already displayed. That's their entire purpose. They're about as open as information gets.
responses.push: You push a label back into its brass holder. It slides in with a satisfying click.
responses.pull: You pull a label from its holder. It comes free with a tiny pop. You feel an irrational pang of guilt.
responses.default: Filing labels. Someone organized this entire archive by hand. Respect.

responses.look: A thick parchment document with a wax seal, filed in Section C-7. The title reads: 'CONTRACT OF PETRIFICATION -- BINDING CURSE (Active).' Below, in small print: 'Clause 47-B: This curse may be terminated through the Rite of Administrative Closure, requiring the Crystal of Mundanity, the Kingdom Administrative Seal, and proper filing.' Someone actually built an exit clause into a curse. Bureaucracy truly is universal.
responses.use: The contract is a legal document. You need it for the Rite of Administrative Closure. It's the curse's own terms and conditions, and you intend to enforce them.
responses.take: You can't take the Original Curse Contract. It's firmly attached to its current situation, much like your growing sense of confusion.
responses.talk: You read the contract aloud. The words hang in the air, faintly humming with residual magic. Somewhere deep in the archive, something shifts. Legal language has power here.
responses.open: The contract is a single rolled parchment. You unroll it, read Clause 47-B again, and roll it back up. The exit clause is still there.
responses.push: You flatten the contract against the shelf. It curls back up immediately. Magical parchment has strong opinions about its preferred shape.
responses.pull: You tug the contract from its filing slot. It resists slightly, as though the archive doesn't want to let it go.

actions.text: You pull the Original Curse Contract from its filing slot. The parchment is warm -- magical documents retain their power, even in a crumbling archive. Clause 47-B is right there in black and white: the curse has an expiration procedure. Someone -- the original caster -- built in a bureaucratic exit clause. This contract is both the problem and the solution. You tuck it carefully into your pack. The archive, having served its purpose for the first time in centuries, seems to exhale.

archive-collapse.title: Death by Bibliography
archive-collapse.narratorText: You pull a stone scroll from the petrified shelves. This is a mistake. The shelves were held together by the weight of their contents, and removing one scroll shifts the balance. The shelf tilts. The one behind it tilts. A domino cascade of stone shelving and fossilized paperwork descends upon you with the enthusiasm of centuries of accumulated knowledge falling on someone who should have known better. You are buried under the weight of every law, decree, and tax record the kingdom ever produced. The archive was held together by the books. Removing one was... structural.

dynamicDescriptions.found_curse_contract: The Royal Archive stands in dusty silence, the gap in Section C-7 where the curse contract used to be glowing faintly with residual magic. You have the document. The answer to the curse was filed correctly all along. Nobody ever checked.
